# Movement

Dominions 6 is a game of provinces. Movement consists of going from one province to another,
and depends on several things: the movement speed of the unit(s), the terrain in the
province, and whether or not the province is friendly or enemy. In general, you can only move
from one province to another if they are connected. To see a province's connections, hit the
`Ctrl-8` key. These connections will be color-coded. It is possible to move through multiple
provinces of enemy territory if your movement allowance is high enough.

The basic concepts regarding movement are as follows:

- Movement takes place as a series of "half-steps": you move out of one province and into
  another.
- When you do this, you pay movement costs for each half-step according to the Movement Cost
  Table.
- Stopping in an enemy province triggers combat, unless all entering units are Stealthy (like
  scouts).
- Armies with multiple units move at the speed of the slowest unit.
- Only commanders may move. Armies may not move if they are not led by a commander.
- Movement into or out of difficult terrain (forest, mountains, swamp) is affected by units'
  survival abilities.

## General rules governing movement

Movement is determined by a unit's map movement factor. If you right-click on any unit, you
will see this factor next to the word "Map Move." A unit with "Map Move 12" has 12 movement
points for moving on the strategic map. Movement is calculated in "half-steps" which must be
made to calculate the cost to leave, and then enter, each province. Each terrain has an
associated movement cost.

| Terrain | Cost |
|---------|-----:|
| Plains | 3 |
| Forest | 5 |
| Waste | 5 |
| Highlands | 6 |
| Swamp | 7 |
| Sea | 5 |
| Cave | 4 |
| Cave Forest | 6 |
| Crystal Cave | 6 |
| Drip Cave | 7 |

: Half step move cost for ground units

These are modified as follows:

- Enemy province (moving unit does not have stealth): +4
- Enemy province (moving unit has stealth): +3
- Snow: +1
- Roads: -2 (min. 2)

Roads are not present on the random maps, but they can be added to custom maps by map
creators. Flying units have different movement costs entirely:

| Terrain | Cost |
|---------|-----:|
| Most terrain | 3 |
| Cave | 5 |
| Enemy province | +1 |

: Half step move cost for fliers

If a unit has a survival ability, such as forest, swamp, waste, or mountains, it moves
through that terrain with a -2 cost. Forest survival also helps when traversing fungus caves,
mountain survival helps when traversing crystal caves and swamp survival helps when
traversing drip caves.

Movement points for troops are based on the following general parameters (each unit has a
specific allowance, but these are typical):

| Unit type | Map Move |
|-----------|---------:|
| Heavy infantry | 8 |
| Light infantry | 14 |
| Light cavalry | 20 |
| Unicorn | 26 |
| Slow flier | 14 |
| Flier | 20 |
| Fast flier | 26 |
| All commanders | +2 |

There are two special terrain types which are properties of the borders between provinces
rather than the provinces themselves: rivers and mountain passes.

- **Rivers** may not be crossed unless the Cold scales in the provinces on both sides of that
  river border are +1 or greater (the river is frozen). Flying, floating, and swimming units
  may cross rivers without penalty regardless of scales, as may units with the ability to
  enter water.
- **Mountain passes** may be crossed if the Heat scales in the provinces on both sides of
  that mountain border are +1 or greater (the passes are open). Flying and floating units may
  cross without penalty regardless of scales, as may units with the Mountain Survival ability.

Additional details:

- Unless a unit is amphibious or aquatic it cannot enter an underwater province. (Poor
  amphibians are included, but differ from amphibians in combat. Amphibians, poor or
  otherwise, may cross rivers without penalty.)
- Aquatic units cannot enter land.
- A unit with the sailing ability may cross water provinces, but may not remain in such a
  province at the end of the turn. This ability does not in itself allow units to cross
  rivers.

All units (both the commander and his troops) must have the abilities listed above in order
to take advantage of them. Just because a commander can fly doesn't mean his troops can.
(Exception: some commanders can give their troops water-breathing abilities, and commanders
with sailing can transport non-sailing units.)

Armies move at the speed of the slowest unit in the army. If you want to quickly remove slow
units from a squad you can mouse-over the squad and hit the `m` key to select the slowest
units in that squad.

> **Example of movement:** Aethelwald the Knight Commander of Avalon is leading his troop of
> knights on a raid from the Canese Plain. He wants to strike into the Atlantian province of
> Ard. He has a map move of 22. Moving from Canese Plain to Trisia costs 3 to leave (reduced
> to 2 by the road), and 3 to enter (reduced to 2). So far he has spent 4. Moving from Trisia
> to Sottera costs 3 to exit (total 7), then 3 for the plains plus 4 because Sottera is an
> enemy province = 7 to enter, total 14. To get to Ard he would have to spend 3 to leave plus
> 4 (enemy), then 3 to enter Ard plus 4 (enemy) = 28 points, and he only has 22. He stops in
> Sottera and will fight the forces of Agartha.

### Stealthy units

Units with the Stealth ability can move unseen from province to province. All movement
restrictions apply normally. Stealthy units ordered to sneak (the default order) move as
normal but do not join in combat if they enter an enemy province that turn. Units already
hiding in an enemy province can be given orders to attack that province in that turn. Certain
special stealthy units can perform other tasks while in an enemy province, such as Instill
Uprising or Assassinate Commander.

Units moving with Stealthy commanders must also be stealthy or the commander loses his
ability to sneak while with them. Stealthy units can be ordered to move normally by issuing
the movement order while holding down `Ctrl`.

Stealthy priests may preach the faith of their pretenders. A preaching stealthy unit gets a
bonus to its stealth dependent on the population of the province (it is easier to hide in a
densely populated province). This stealth bonus is only available while performing the Preach
order.

### Glamour and Darkvision

**Glamour** units cannot be seen at all when in a friendly province. Glamour also gives units
the Mirror Image ability in combat.

**Darkvision** units are less affected by darkness, depending on the level of their
Darkvision attribute. Penalties for darkness affect a unit's Attack, Defense, and Precision.
For normal units these are reduced as follows: night -3, Utterdark -6, blind -9. Darkvision
does not help you if you are blind.

## Initiation of combat

Combat in Dominions 6 is resolved between any mutually discovered enemy forces in a province
where neither side has the benefit of a fortress. This means you can have a scout or spy in a
province with the enemy, but if he remains undiscovered, combat is not initiated. If one side
is in a fortress, it cannot be assailed until the fortress is besieged and broken (see
[Sieges](06-combat.qmd)), or the defending force chooses to sortie (Break Siege).

**More than two enemies in combat.** If forces from different sides occupy the same province
at the end of a turn (and neither one is in a fortress), they fight. If three or more sides
have forces in a province, the game determines in which order they entered, and fights the
battles sequentially, with the survivors of each battle being the defenders in the next.
There will never be more than two sides in a single battle.

> **Example:** Jotunheim, Marignon, and Pythium all enter Robber Home in the same turn. The
> game determines that Marignon got there first, followed by Pythium and Jotunheim. The first
> battle is Pythium attacking Marignon. Whichever side wins then defends against Jotunheim,
> but only with those units that survived the first battle.

Armies which are allied can occupy the same province. If allied armies are attacked, the
attacker fights each defender sequentially in random order.

## Orders

Units can be given a variety of orders. A movement order is given by left-clicking on the
commander's icon, then left-clicking on the destination province. This arrow is
point-to-point and does not mean the moving unit will enter each province the arrow crosses.
If the move is illegal, no arrow is displayed. If the move order is legal but becomes illegal
before hosting (e.g. removing Flying Boots from a commander crossing difficult terrain), the
arrow remains but the commander will not move, because the move order is validated during turn
hosting.

**Move**
: Move to another province. If it is an enemy province, combat will occur. A commander given
an order to move to a province containing a fort will enter the fort (contrast with Move and
Patrol).

**Sneak**
: Movement into enemy provinces without being detected. Only Stealthy units can attempt such
moves. Their chance of success depends on their Stealth rating, the number of units trying to
hide, and the number of units looking for them. Combat will not occur unless the unit is
discovered. To give a stealthy commander the order to move normally (and thus attack the
province it enters), hold down `Ctrl` while left-clicking. A stealthy commander will not
sneak unless all units under his command are stealthy.

**Patrol**
: Patrolling a province both reduces unrest and has a chance to discover hidden units. For
each point of unrest eliminated by patrollers, the population is reduced by 10. If an enemy is
discovered by a patrol, the entire defending force of the province will engage it. If there
is a castle, those inside without a patrol order will not participate. Patrol Strength is
affected by unrest:
  - **Stealth strength:** Stealth value of leader -1 per stealthy unit in his army (or -0.5
    if unit has >=+50 in stealth).
  - **Patrol Strength:** Sum of Patrol strength of all patrolling units - unrest/2 (capped at
    unrest 100) + (province defence -14 if province defence is 15 or greater).
  - The stealthy units are found if Patrol Strength + 2d25 (open-ended) > Stealth strength +
    2d25 (open-ended).
  - The individual Patrol Strength of a unit = (Precision + (Map Move, or 30 if flying)) / 20.
    Units with a Patrol bonus add it. Commanders have their value doubled; undisciplined and
    mindless units count as half.

**Defend**
: Exactly the same as Patrol except there is no patrolling. Units enter combat against any
non-Stealthy units entering the province. Unrest is not reduced, but the province is still
defended. This is the default order. Units ordered to defend in a province with a fort will
not fight an enemy force entering the province; instead they become besieged. (The difference
between Patrol and Defend in a province with a fort: patrollers engage in combat outside the
fort, while defenders concede the province and become besieged.)

Movement is carried out in two steps: first, all units moving to a friendly province do so;
afterward, movement takes place in which units are moving to an enemy province. Thus, if you
are moving an army to one of your provinces and an enemy is moving to that same province, your
army will get there first.

> **Important:** If you order a force into a friendly province containing a fortress, it will
> automatically "enter" the fortress and behave as though it has the order "Defend Castle."
> If you want a second army to battle in the province itself rather than going into the
> fortress, you must use **Move and Patrol**.

**Move and Patrol**
: Becomes available once a unit has been given an order to move into a friendly province with
a fortress. It instructs the commander to move to the province and then patrol outside the
fortress. Enemy units entering will be engaged in combat. You can also hold `shift`+`ctrl`
when selecting the destination. Next turn the order is replaced by a regular Patrol order.

**Blood Hunt**
: How blood slaves are collected for blood magic sacrifices. A blood hunter must pass three
successive checks:
  - A level check: % chance of success = 10 + (blood level x 30)
  - A population check: % chance of success = province's population / 75
  - An unrest check: % chance of failure = province unrest / 4

  If all succeed, the number of slaves found is d6 + blood level and unrest increases by
  d(slaves x 3 + 4). If any check fails, no slaves are found and unrest increases by d6 - 1.
  Magic site frequency other than 50% also affects the number found (every 5% adds or
  subtracts on average 0.5 slaves). Beware: some people may refuse to give up their daughters,
  which can cause a fight. A strong dominion convinces commoners it is an honor to be
  sacrificed; at dominion strength 10 almost no one refuses.

**Break Siege**
: Orders units besieged in a fortress to fight a battle against the enemy units in that
province. Units which retreat flee back into the castle, or into a neighboring friendly
province (50/50 if both are possible).

**Maintain Siege**
: The default order for any units besieging a fortress. Only units with this order contribute
to siege strength.

**Storm Castle**
: Only available if you have reduced a castle's defenses to zero. On the next turn you may
storm the castle, initiating a battle in which the defenders begin behind their own walls.
Because castle storming occurs after movement, a besieging force may be forced to fight an
enemy relieving force first.

**Assassinate**
: A random enemy commander in that province is targeted. The assassin fights a battle with
the commander, plus any guards or townsfolk nearby. Bodyguards explicitly assigned to the
victim have a 50% chance each of being present (increased by the Bodyguard ability, decreased
by the Assassin's patience value). The target is considered unprepared and will not follow
scripted orders. Assassinations occur at random locations based on the buildings present and
who the victim is (e.g. a priest in the temple, a normal commander at the tavern).

**Scale Walls**
: Assassins can usually operate in all provinces they are sneaking in, even provinces with
forts. But if the fort is under siege, it is not possible to assassinate into or out of the
castle. The Scale Walls ability negates this protection. Flying and teleporting units can
likewise ignore walls. The same restriction applies to Seduction, Lure, Infiltration, etc.

**Seduction**
: Some units (Nagini, dryads) can seduce enemy commanders of the opposite gender to switch
sides. The victim must succeed at a morale check or become seduced. The seducer must be
adjacent to a friendly province into which the target is taken (a flying seducer need not be
adjacent). Items that grant beauty make seduction harder to resist. A failed seduction may
result in an assassination battle.

**Dream Seduction**
: Like seduction, but with a difficult magic resistance roll and usually a higher morale
check difficulty. Succubi have this power and benefit from items boosting beauty (vs morale)
and penetration (vs magic resistance).

**Corruption**
: Some units can corrupt enemy commanders to switch sides. Works like seduction, but beauty
items have no effect.

**Lure of the sirens**
: A difficult magic resistance check followed by a morale check against the lure ability (10).
Usable only in coastal provinces. Failure doesn't cause an assassination battle, making it
rather safe. If successful, the target drowns himself, or enters the sea and attacks the
siren if able to breathe underwater.

**Preach**
: Allows priests to increase dominion in a province (see the [Dominion chapter](08-dominion.qmd)).

**Pillage**
: Increases unrest and kills population, decreases the province's supplies, and gains gold and
food for the pillaging army. Fast and large units are better; barbarians and units with Fear
are exceptionally good. The supplies gained last only one month.

**Raid**
: A move that can only be performed by a commander with the Pillager ability if his army has a
map move of 20 or more. It does a movement followed by a pillage (at reduced strength: only
units with the pillager ability contribute, and their pillage strength is divided by 2). A
raid will often result in a fight, which must be won for any pillaging to take effect.

**Reanimate**
: Available to many undead priests, some nations (Ermor, Lemuria), and some units (Carrion
Lords and their servants). Most reanimator priests can choose what kind of undead they
reanimate. Ghouls are living humans transformed by vile rites (reduces population). Soulless
are reanimated corpses of the newly dead (requires unburied corpses; reduces them). Longdead
are reanimated skeletal warriors (no limit). The table below lists the reanimation rate for
Ermor - Ashen Empire.

| Priest level | Ghouls | Soulless | Longdead | Longdead horsemen | Lictors |
|-------------:|-------:|---------:|---------:|------------------:|--------:|
| 1 | 6 | 8 | 3 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 7 | 16 | 5 | 1 | 0 |
| 3 | 8 | 24 | 7 | 2 | 1 |
| 4 | 9 | 32 | 9 | 3 | 2 |
| 5 | 10 | 40 | 11 | 4 | 3 |

: Reanimation table (Ermor - Ashen Empire)

**Manikin Reanimation**
: Asphodel gets manikins and different carrion beasts instead of normal undead. If there are
still human inhabitants or dead human corpses in the province, there is a chance for manikins
and mandragoras to appear; otherwise carrion beasts (based on animals) are reanimated.
Reanimate Manikins is much less effective in an enemy dominion.

**Contact Allies**
: Some commanders (e.g. Draconians) are able to gather more troops by spending the month
gathering them.

**Perform Blood Sacrifice**
: May only be performed in a province with a temple, by a nation which has the ability because
it has been part of their history:
  - *Early age:* Mictlan, Xibalba, Marverni, Sauromatia, Abysia, Pangaea, Vanheim, Hinnom,
    Berytos
  - *Middle age:* Abysia, Vanheim, Pyrene, Vanarus, Nidavangr
  - *Late age:* Marignon, Mictlan, Xibalba, Abysia, Midgard, Gath

**Capture Slaves**
: Available to Mictlan and Nazca. A 1d6 + 5 number of slaves are rounded up and forced to join
the army. They are weak and cowardly soldiers but are free to recruit.

**Become Prophet**
: Designates the unit as the one and only prophet of the nation's pretender god. The unit's
priest level is increased by 1 or to 3, whichever is higher, and the prophet starts spreading
dominion like a temple. If a prophet is killed, the nation must wait half a year (6 turns)
before designating a new one.

**Instill Uprising**
: Available to units with the spy ability (spies, bards). Increases the level of unrest in the
enemy province they occupy.

**Infiltrate**
: Infiltrate the enemy capital to access records and find out as much as possible about the
nation. Only in the capital of an enemy nation. If successful it reveals the mundane score
graphs (plus magic graphs if the spy is a caster, plus the dominion graph if a priest). The
attempt has about 50% chance to fail, after which the enemy learns someone attempted to
infiltrate.

**Hide**
: The default order for stealthy units who are not moving. A hiding unit will not participate
in any combat in that province unless discovered, in which case a separate battle is fought.

**Attack Current Province**
: Available to Stealthy units hiding in an enemy province. The unit joins friendly units
attacking the province. Treated as a movement order of distance zero.

**Construct Building**
: In any friendly province, three types of buildings can be constructed: a fort (anyone), a
temple (requires a sacred commander), and a lab (requires a mage).

**Demolish Building**
: Can be done by anyone and takes one month, whether a fort or a lab. Temples cannot be
demolished (but enemy temples are destroyed automatically when conquered). A fort cannot be
demolished if it is under siege.

## Turn resolution sequence

All players' turn orders are resolved simultaneously when a turn is hosted. During hosting,
the game resolves orders in the following order:

1. **Send messages** -- gold, gems, and items are dispatched (always, since everything that
   could stop this occurs later).
2. **Research** -- mages perform research. Even if a mage is later killed, he contributes his
   research points that turn.
3. **Recruitment** -- new units and commanders are recruited.
4. **Empowerment** -- increased magic paths due to Empowerment are conferred.
5. **Forge items** -- new items are forged into the national inventory.
6. **Preach** -- priests preach and dominion is adjusted.
7. **Heretic preaching** -- heretics, insane commanders and commanders with shattered soul
   preach.
8. **Claim thrones** -- Thrones of Ascension are claimed.
9. **Quick special orders** -- a few special orders are quicker (Enter Site to Scry, Cultivate
   Pearls).
10. **Magic rituals** -- all mages cast their rituals in random order.
11. **Remote attacks** -- rituals that strike enemy armies in remote provinces (e.g. Fires
    from Afar).
12. **Magic battles** -- battles caused by magic (e.g. Wind Ride, teleporting).
13. **Lost in other planes** -- units lost in another plane, including battles fought there.
14. **Site searches** -- magic site searches are resolved.
15. **Prophets** -- prophets are declared.
16. **Call God** -- priests call their banished gods.
17. **Awakening** -- pretenders awaken (dormant or imprisoned).
18. **Blood hunting** -- the hunt for blood slaves takes place.
19. **Horrors** -- units are visited by Horrors, if such a misfortune befalls them.
20. **Assassinations** -- assassination attempts (including all seduction and spy abilities).
21. **Relinquish province** -- relinquish to a non-stealthed allied commander present.
22. **Claim mounts** -- commanders without mounts can claim new ones.
23. **Lone mounts** -- riders without mounts or smart mounts without riders try to make their
    way home or disperse.
24. **Friendly movement** -- all movement ending in a friendly province.
25. **Other movement** -- all other movement, including Break Siege.
26. **Resolve battles** -- battle resolution from movement.
27. **Castle storming** -- castles are stormed and battles resolved.
28. **Global enchantments** -- take effect (casting itself happened in step 10).
29. **Random events** -- Fortune/Misfortune events.
30. **Resolve battles** -- battles caused by events.
31. **Magic items/monsters** -- special effects from magic items or monsters.
32. **Resolve battles** -- battles caused by the previous.
33. **Sneak discovery** -- discovered stealthy units fight for their lives.
34. **Change besieger** -- if two allies besiege the same castle, the besieger is decided
    (larger armies take precedence).
35. **Building construction** -- fortresses, temples, and labs are built or demolished.
36. **Special orders** -- Reanimate, Summon Allies, etc. (allies summoned this turn are not
    available for this turn's battles).
37. **Pillage** -- increases unrest and kills population.
38. **Income** -- nations collect income (after Pillage, so pillaging a conquered province
    yields reduced or no income).
39. **Unrest alterations** -- changes from dominion, scales, and patrolling.
40. **Starvation** -- units without supplies suffer starvation (after battles, so the first
    turn without supplies an army fights without starvation effects).
41. **Upkeep / Desertion** -- upkeep is paid (after income) and desertion happens.
42. **Dominion spread** -- all dominion spread is conducted.
43. **Dominion effects** -- population death, insanity, spreading heat or cold, etc.
44. **Site effects** -- magic sites spread disease, unrest, and the like.
45. **Overpopulation** -- if close to the limit of 600,000 units or 50,000 commanders, the
    most numerous are culled.
46. **Aging** -- only during midwinter. Units age one year and may get an affliction.
47. **Resolve battles** -- leftover battles from previous events.
48. **Heal / Disease** -- units regain lost hit points unless diseased, in which case they
    suffer more damage.
49. **Insanity** -- units may go insane from certain dominion or other effects.
50. **Mercenaries** -- mercenaries are bought or maintained.
51. **New random heroes** -- heroes may appear at a nation's capital gates.
